metadata.summary: Run LLMs locally via Ollama's HTTP API (port 11434). Primary use: privacy-sensitive workloads, offline environments, and cost elimination for high-volume low-stakes tasks.

content.title: Local LLM with Ollama
content.summary: Run LLMs locally via Ollama's HTTP API (port 11434). Primary use: privacy-sensitive workloads, offline environments, and cost elimination for high-volume low-stakes tasks. The OpenAI-compatible `/v1` endpoint allows swapping between local and cloud APIs by changing only `base_url` and `api_key`, enabling local development + cloud production without code changes.

why.p: Cloud APIs send content to external servers and have per-token costs and rate limits. Local inference eliminates both at the cost of hardware. For classifying/summarizing large volumes of internal documents, local models can be 100x cheaper. The OpenAI-compatible API means zero code changes to switch local vs. cloud for testing.

list.item: Data privacy requirements prohibiting external API calls
High-volume, low-stakes tasks where cloud API costs are prohibitive
Offline or air-gapped environments
Development/testing — no API costs, no rate limits, instant iteration
Deploying a custom fine-tuned model not hosted externally
Latency-sensitive applications where local inference beats network round-trip
Tasks requiring frontier-level reasoning (complex code, multi-step math) — local 7B/13B models underperform Opus/GPT-4o
Machine has <8GB RAM — models will page to disk and be unusably slow
Production services with unpredictable load spikes — local GPU is not elastically scalable
Latest model capabilities needed — local model libraries lag cloud providers by months

section.name: setup-usage
section.p: Installation, RAM-based model selection, health check pattern, and OpenAI-compatible endpoint for local/cloud switching.

rule.statement: Keep Ollama running as a systemd service on Linux; check server availability via /api/tags at agent startup and fail fast with a clear error if Ollama is not running.
rule.rationale: Agents that assume Ollama is running will produce cryptic connection errors. A health check at startup with a descriptive error message saves debug time.

example.title: Installation and server startup
example.code:
# Install (Linux) curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
# Start server (keep as systemd service in production) ollama serve
# Pull a model (blocking — handle in agent as a blocking operation) ollama pull llama3.1:8b
# List available models ollama list

example.title: Health check and model call pattern

section.name: openai-compatible-endpoint
section.p: Use the OpenAI-compatible /v1 endpoint for all agent code to enable local/cloud switching by changing only base_url and api_key, with zero code changes.

rule.statement: Use the OpenAI-compatible /v1 endpoint for all agent code — it enables local/cloud switching by changing only base_url and api_key, with zero code changes.
rule.rationale: Maintaining two code paths (Ollama API + OpenAI API) doubles testing surface. The /v1 compatibility layer makes Ollama a drop-in replacement for development.

example.title: OpenAI client with local/cloud switching

section.name: model-selection-by-ram
section.p: Choose model size based on available VRAM. Quantized variants reduce memory by 50-60% with minimal quality loss.

list.item: phi3:mini (2.3GB) — 4GB RAM, lightweight tasks
llama3.1:8b (4.7GB) — 8GB RAM, general purpose, supports tool use
mistral:7b (4.1GB) — 8GB RAM, fast, efficient
codellama:13b (7.4GB) — 16GB RAM, code generation
mixtral:8x7b (26GB) — 32GB RAM, mixture of experts
llama3.1:70b (40GB) — 48GB RAM, complex reasoning

rule.statement: Use quantized variants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) — they cut VRAM by 50–60% with 2–5% quality loss; log eval_count and total_duration to detect GPU fallback to CPU (10–50x slower).
rule.rationale: Unquantized models that exceed VRAM fall back to CPU inference, which is 10–50x slower. eval_count/total_duration expose this without needing nvidia-smi in the agent.

section.name: modelfiles-and-production
section.p: Bake system prompts and parameters into Modelfiles, deploy with Docker GPU passthrough, and understand critical concurrency and tool-use limitations.

rule.statement: Use Modelfiles to bake system prompts and parameters into a named model (ollama create task-agent -f Modelfile); this avoids re-sending system prompts on every call and sets context size explicitly.
rule.rationale: Default context window (num_ctx) is model-dependent and often too small for production. Sending large system prompts on every call wastes tokens. Modelfiles bake both into the model artifact.

example.title: Modelfile for a code assistant
example.code: # Modelfile for a code assistant FROM codellama:13b PARAMETER temperature 0.2 PARAMETER num_ctx 8192 SYSTEM """You are an expert Python programmer. Write clean, documented code with error handling."""
example.note: Build with: ollama create python-coder -f Modelfile

rule.statement: Ollama does not support parallel request processing by default — design agents to expect serialized local inference, not parallelized; never send concurrent requests expecting parallel execution.
rule.rationale: An agent pool sending concurrent requests will serialize them, not parallelize. Designing for parallelism leads to unexpected queueing and latency spikes in production.

rule.statement: Use /api/chat (not /api/generate) for tool use / function calling; only llama3.1 and mistral-nemo support it — check model compatibility before designing tool-calling agents.
rule.rationale: The /api/generate endpoint does not support tool use. Older Mistral models do not implement function calling. Tool-calling agents must verify model support at startup.

antipattern.description: Hardcoding the model name in agent code (e.g., always using "llama3.1:8b").
antipattern.reason: Different machines have different VRAM. A hardcoded model that exceeds available VRAM will fail or run on CPU. Model name must be configurable via environment or config file.

example.title: Docker Compose with GPU passthrough
example.code: # docker-compose.yml for Ollama with GPU passthrough services: ollama: image: ollama/ollama ports: - "11434:11434" volumes: - ollama_data:/root/.ollama deploy: resources: reservations: devices: - driver: nvidia count: all capabilities: [gpu] volumes: ollama_data:

section.name: agent-integration-gotchas
section.p: Critical limitations and best practices for agentic workflows using Ollama.

list.item: RAM ceiling is hard: a 13B model requires ~16GB RAM; exceeding it causes OOM or extreme slowdown
GPU detection can fail silently — Ollama may fall back to CPU without warning; always check inference speed
Context window of local models is typically 4K–8K tokens by default (configured via `num_ctx` in Modelfile); cloud models offer 128K–2M
Ollama server must be running before the agent starts — no auto-start by default on Linux
Model pull during first use adds a blocking delay of minutes; agents must handle this gracefully
Tool use / function calling support varies by model family — llama3.1 supports it; older mistral does not
Ollama does not support parallel request processing by default — an agent pool sending concurrent requests will serialize, not parallelize
`eval_count` and `total_duration` in the response let you compute tokens/second; log these to detect GPU fallback (CPU is 10–50x slower)
Model names must match exactly including the tag (e.g., `llama3.1:8b` not `llama3.1`) — wrong name returns 404 with no helpful message
Agents that pull models on first use must handle the pull as a blocking operation with streaming status — the pull JSONL stream ends with `"status": "success"`
Local model context windows are small — an agent that passes a long conversation history will silently truncate it; monitor `eval_count` for signs of truncation

section.name: agentic-workflow-pattern
section.p: An agent using Ollama should check server availability via `/api/tags` at startup and fail fast with a clear error if Ollama is not running. Model selection should be configurable (not hardcoded) so the agent works on different hardware. The OpenAI-compatible endpoint (`/v1`) lets agents swap between Ollama and cloud APIs by changing only `base_url` and `api_key`, making local/cloud switching seamless for testing vs. production.
section.note: Recommended subagent: `faion-sdd-executor-agent` — can be configured to use Ollama for local SDD task drafts before sending to cloud for review.

list.item: Match model size to available VRAM: 7B/8B needs 6–8GB, 13B needs 12–16GB, 70B needs 40GB+
Use quantized variants (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M) — they cut VRAM by 50–60% with 2–5% quality loss
Set `num_ctx` in Modelfile explicitly — default is model-dependent and often too small for production
Keep Ollama running as a systemd service on Linux so agents don't need to start it
Use Modelfiles to bake system prompts into a named model (`ollama create task-agent -f Modelfile`) — avoids re-sending system prompts per call
Set `timeout=120` or higher for large models; default HTTP timeouts will fail on first token generation
For concurrent agent requests, Ollama queues them — design agents to expect serialized local inference

template.purpose: Production-ready OllamaService class with health check, generate, and pull methods.
